result.markdown:
---
title: "5. Portfolio construction, diversification, and risk"
---



Identifying return sources is necessary but not sufficient. A portfolio built from well-understood return sources can still fail if the positions are sized incorrectly, if the exposures are more correlated than they appear, if the path of returns is too difficult to hold through, or if concentration accumulates silently through market drift. Portfolio construction is the activity that addresses these problems. It is not a secondary layer added after the investment work is done. It is part of the investment work.

## Portfolios are systems of covarying exposures

Markowitz's foundational insight was that the relevant measure for evaluating any asset is not its expected return and variance in isolation, but its contribution to the distribution of outcomes for the portfolio as a whole [@markowitz1952]. An asset with high expected return but strong positive covariance with existing holdings may add less value than its standalone characteristics suggest. An asset with modest expected return and low or negative covariance with the rest of the portfolio may add considerably more. This is the central reason why portfolio construction cannot be reduced to ranking assets by expected return and allocating proportionally.

The practical implication is that investors who evaluate assets one at a time — forming a thesis at the security level and then adding the position to the portfolio as if it were entering a container — are systematically underestimating concentration risk. A portfolio is not a container. It is a system of covarying exposures whose behavior under stress is frequently different from the sum of its parts evaluated separately.

A concrete example makes this visible. An equity portfolio with forty positions across eight sectors may appear to have substantial breadth. If twenty-five of those forty positions are growth-oriented technology businesses — labeled as software, semiconductors, internet platforms, or tech-enabled industrials — the actual number of distinct exposures is far smaller than the position count suggests. A repricing of long-duration growth assets, as occurred through 2022 when U.S. ten-year real yields rose approximately 250 basis points, can damage the entire portfolio simultaneously regardless of how many ticker symbols it contains. The concentration was in the discount rate sensitivity of the underlying businesses, not in their sector labels.

Measuring genuine diversification requires looking through surface labels to the underlying factor exposures and asking whether those exposures are correlated in the stress states that matter most. Two assets are genuinely diversifying if they provide return through different mechanisms, fail under different conditions, and do not both depend on the same macro conditions persisting. By that standard, the set of genuinely uncorrelated return sources available in most investable universes is considerably smaller than the number of available asset classes.

## Correlation is backward-looking and regime-dependent

Correlation is the most widely used input in portfolio construction and also one of the most frequently misused. The limitation is not statistical precision — it is that correlation is backward-looking by construction and regime-dependent in ways that matter most precisely when investors are most reliant on it.

Correlation summarizes co-movement over a chosen historical period. It does not explain why that co-movement occurred, whether the same forces will persist, or how the relationship will behave in environments that differ from the sample. Two assets may appear uncorrelated across a long history because they responded to different shocks during the measurement window, not because their underlying drivers are structurally independent. A new shock that affects both simultaneously — an inflation surge, a liquidity crisis, a sharp rise in discount rates — can tighten correlations rapidly across assets that previously appeared diversifying.

The more fundamental problem is that correlations tend to rise in stress periods — exactly when investors are most dependent on diversification holding. In calm markets, idiosyncratic return drivers, differences in carry, and microstructure noise can keep assets separated. In stress, the dominant driver often becomes broad deleveraging: forced selling, margin calls, redemptions, and the withdrawal of liquidity, which affects nearly all risk assets simultaneously. The chart below demonstrates this empirically using the Fama-French market factor and bond and credit returns from 1990 to 2023. For both equity-bond and equity-credit pairs, the correlation in stress periods — defined as the top quartile of rolling equity volatility — is substantially higher than in calm periods. The equity-credit correlation in particular moves from mildly positive in calm periods to strongly positive in stress, confirming that high-yield credit provides much less diversification than its investment-grade label might imply when it is most needed.

::: {#fd5dde36 .cell execution_count=2}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Pearson correlations between equity and bonds (left) and equity and high-yield credit (right), split into full sample, calm periods (bottom 75% of rolling equity volatility), and stress periods (top 25%). In both cases correlations rise materially in stress — when investors are most reliant on diversification working. Source: Ken French Data Library, FRED, author calculations.](04_portfolio_construction_files/figure-html/cell-3-output-1.png){width=883 height=387}
:::
:::


Relying on unconditional correlation estimates to design a portfolio that must survive stress is therefore making a systematic error: the number being used is most accurate during the periods when it matters least. A portfolio built to survive a scenario in which correlations rise across all risk assets is structurally more honest than one whose diversification claims rest entirely on a decade of favorable covariance.

## Position sizing determines how much of an edge is actually captured

If concentration and correlation are taken seriously, position sizing becomes one of the most consequential decisions in portfolio construction — and one that receives far less analytical attention than asset selection.

Size determines how much of the expected return can actually be captured and how much damage an error can do. A correct thesis expressed at 1 percent of the portfolio produces a negligible contribution to return even if the position doubles. The same thesis expressed at 20 percent produces a material outcome in both directions. This asymmetry means that sizing errors are often more consequential than analytical errors, particularly in concentrated portfolios where one or two mistakes can overwhelm the contribution of everything else.

Several frameworks exist for thinking about position sizing rigorously. Kelly-criterion-based approaches size positions in proportion to the ratio of expected edge to variance, maximizing long-run compound growth — but they are extremely sensitive to estimation error and produce sizes that are too aggressive for most institutional constraints [@thorp2006]. Mean-variance optimization provides a theoretically optimal size for a given set of return and covariance estimates, but it is similarly sensitive to input error and tends to concentrate the entire portfolio in a small number of assets when inputs are uncertain — a problem known as corner solutions [@markowitz1952].

In practice, thoughtful portfolio construction involves a pragmatic combination: a base sizing framework that reflects conviction and factor contribution, modified by explicit constraints on maximum position size, maximum factor concentration, and minimum liquidity buffer. What distinguishes a well-constructed portfolio from a poorly constructed one is not which framework is used but whether the sizing decisions are made explicitly, with awareness of how wrong they could be, rather than implicitly as a residual of the selection process.

The table below makes the sizing decision concrete. For a given strategy with known volatility and a win-loss ratio of 1.5 — meaning winning positions are on average 50 percent larger than losing ones — the required hit rate to be profitable is fixed regardless of position size. What changes with size is the portfolio volatility contribution and the position-level loss required to breach a 2-standard-deviation portfolio event. These numbers show why a position that is analytically identical can be acceptable at 2 percent and damaging at 15 percent.

::: {#b3d881d6 .cell execution_count=3}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![The same investment thesis expressed at three position sizes: 2%, 8%, and 15% of portfolio. Portfolio vol = 12%, thesis vol = 25%, correlation with portfolio = 0.30, win/loss ratio = 1.5x. The required hit rate is the same at all three sizes — it is set by the win/loss ratio. What changes is the portfolio vol contribution and the maximum tolerable position-level loss before a 2-sigma portfolio event is triggered.](04_portfolio_construction_files/figure-html/cell-4-output-1.png){width=883 height=255}
:::
:::


## Path risk: the portfolio that cannot be held

The most practically important concept in portfolio construction that receives the least theoretical attention is path risk — the risk not that the portfolio produces a poor terminal outcome, but that the experience of holding it through an adverse period forces an exit before the terminal outcome is realized.

Portfolios are not evaluated at a single future point in time. They are lived through continuously. Investors face interim mark-to-market losses, client redemptions, committee pressure, investment policy constraints, and the psychological difficulty of maintaining conviction through extended drawdowns. A portfolio that is theoretically optimal over a ten-year horizon but produces a 40 percent drawdown in the third year may fail in practice — not because the thesis was wrong but because the drawdown exceeded the tolerance for loss, triggered redemptions, or forced a change in the investment process at exactly the wrong moment.

Volatility measures the dispersion of outcomes around an average. Drawdown measures the lived experience of loss — the peak-to-trough decline and the duration for which capital is below its previous high. For most investors, drawdown is the more practically relevant risk. A portfolio with moderate volatility but a long, grinding underwater period can be extremely difficult to hold, particularly when the recovery requires continuing to own positions that have already been painful. Ilmanen, Israel, Moskowitz, Thapar, and van den Heuvel document this empirically: across a century of drawdown episodes, strategies that appeared similar on average-return metrics differed substantially in their drawdown profiles and in the behavioral challenges they created for investors trying to maintain allocation through stress [@ilmanen2018].

The Kelly criterion literature makes a related point. At full Kelly sizing, the strategy maximizes long-run compound growth in expectation, but the variance of the path is large enough that most individual realizations fall well short of the expected compound return. Fractional Kelly — sizing at half or a third of the theoretically optimal size — sacrifices some expected return but substantially improves the practical survivability of the strategy [@thorp2006]. The reduction in expected return is often smaller than the reduction in drawdown severity.

The chart below illustrates this using two simulated portfolios designed to have similar annualized Sharpe ratios. Portfolio A experiences a sharp but short crisis — the kind of drawdown that is painful but resolves quickly, allowing the investor to recover and continue. Portfolio B experiences a long shallow drawdown lasting several years. From a summary statistics perspective, both look similar. From a practical holding perspective, they are very different: Portfolio B tests institutional patience for a period that is long enough to trigger mandate reviews, client redemptions, and personnel changes. The probability that the portfolio is abandoned before the recovery occurs is materially higher for Portfolio B despite identical long-run statistics.

::: {#82923265 .cell execution_count=4}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Two simulated portfolios with similar annualized Sharpe ratios but very different drawdown profiles. Portfolio A experiences a sharp but short crisis. Portfolio B experiences a long shallow drawdown over several years. Identical summary statistics correspond to radically different investor experiences and very different probabilities of being abandoned before recovery. Source: Author simulation.](04_portfolio_construction_files/figure-html/cell-5-output-1.png){width=826 height=499}
:::
:::


The practical implication for portfolio design is that drawdown budgeting should be as explicit as return targeting. A portfolio designed with a stated tolerance for a 15 percent maximum drawdown should be tested against whether its actual drawdown experience across stressed scenarios is consistent with that tolerance before capital is committed.

## Rebalancing as structural risk control

A portfolio left to market drift will gradually become a different portfolio from the one that was constructed. Positions that have appreciated become larger, positions that have declined become smaller, and the factor exposures of the original design shift in ways that often increase rather than reduce concentration — because assets that have already performed strongly tend to dominate an unmanaged portfolio and often carry more demanding valuations and more crowded positioning than when they were first added.

Rebalancing is the mechanism by which the intended portfolio structure is maintained against this drift. Its primary role is not to capture mean reversion — though it has historically provided a modest return contribution in certain asset class combinations. Its primary role is risk control: ensuring that the portfolio continues to express the intended exposures in the intended proportions rather than allowing market drift to silently concentrate risk in recent winners.

::: {#b74fd8ed .cell execution_count=5}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Equity weight drift in a nominally 60/40 portfolio during the 2010–2021 U.S. equity bull market. Without rebalancing, the equity allocation drifted well above 80 percent by the end of the period. Annual rebalancing maintained the target. Source: FRED (SP500, GS10).](04_portfolio_construction_files/figure-html/cell-6-output-1.png){width=883 height=403}
:::
:::


For systematic strategies, the rebalancing question is particularly acute because the signal driving each position has a finite half-life. A momentum signal degrades over time as the information it reflects becomes stale. Rebalancing at a frequency that is too low relative to signal decay means the portfolio holds positions whose original rationale has already weakened. Rebalancing too frequently relative to signal persistence incurs unnecessary turnover costs that erode the premium being harvested. The optimal frequency is therefore a function of the strategy's specific signal economics, not a fixed calendar choice.

## Combining signals: the portfolio is stronger than its parts

The portfolio construction principles above apply equally to discretionary and systematic portfolios, but the systematic context adds a dimension that deserves explicit treatment. When multiple signals or strategies are combined, the combination itself becomes a portfolio problem, and the same questions about covariance, concentration, and path risk apply at the strategy level.

Many factors and signals that appear modest or marginal on a standalone basis become substantially more attractive when combined with other factors that are lowly correlated with them. The chart below shows this using the Fama-French value and momentum factors. Both HML and UMD have produced positive standalone Sharpe ratios over the full sample. But the 50/50 combination produces a Sharpe ratio that exceeds the weighted average of the two components — because the two factors are negatively correlated with each other. Momentum tends to perform worst precisely when value performs best, and vice versa. The rolling correlation panel on the right confirms that this negative relationship is persistent across most of the sample period, not a result specific to one period.

::: {#30fd33b8 .cell execution_count=6}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Left: annualized Sharpe ratios for Fama-French HML (value), UMD (momentum), the weighted average of the two components, and the 50/50 combination. The combination Sharpe exceeds the weighted average because the two factors are negatively correlated. Right: rolling 60-month correlation between HML and UMD, showing the persistent negative relationship. Source: Ken French Data Library, monthly, 1963–present.](04_portfolio_construction_files/figure-html/cell-7-output-1.png){width=883 height=387}
:::
:::


This property — that the portfolio of signals is better than the average signal — is the systematic equivalent of what Markowitz observed about individual assets, and it is the most important structural reason for combining multiple signals rather than concentrating on the single strongest one. The implication for a research process is that signal evaluation should always include portfolio context. A signal that is only marginally attractive on a standalone basis may still deserve capital if its correlation to existing signals is low. A signal that looks impressive in isolation may deserve a smaller allocation if it introduces concentrated exposure to a risk the rest of the portfolio already carries. How much a signal improves the risk-adjusted return of the overall portfolio — its incremental contribution — matters more than its standalone performance, and those two quantities frequently point in opposite directions [@asness2013].

The portfolio construction principles in this section — understanding the system rather than the collection, recognizing that correlation rises in stress, sizing explicitly rather than implicitly, designing for path survivability, rebalancing to maintain intended structure, and combining signals for their portfolio contribution — provide the practical foundation for translating the return sources identified in section 4 into a portfolio that can actually be held through the conditions it will encounter. Section 6 then addresses the remaining question: what should that portfolio be expected to return from here?
